sentence: Subjective:
The patient, a 54-year-old male, presents with a 2-3 month history of stabbing, shooting pain in the right cheekbone area of his face.
He reports the pain is exacerbated by certain temperatures, cold wind, and occasionally by pressure.
He describes the pain as excruciating, rating it a 10 out of 10 when it occurs.
He has tried ibuprofen, Motrin, and tiger balm without relief.
He has a history of occasional headaches, but no migraines.
He denies numbness or tingling in the affected area.
Past medical history:
The patient has a history of occasional headaches, but no migraines or other significant medical conditions.
Review of systems:
The patient reports no other symptoms in other organs or systems.
Current medications:
I prescribed Tylenol for occasional headaches.
Objective:
Vital signs: [NOT MENTIONED]
Physical exam: The patient's facial symmetry appears normal.
He is able to follow my finger in both directions and can smile symmetrically.
He can hear equally well on both sides of his face.
The patient reports a little numbness on the right side of his face when the doctor runs his fingers along the side of his face, but no significant weakness.
The doctor pokes the patient's face with a needle and notes that the right side feels dull and the left side feels sharp.
Assessment / Problem List:
The patient has trigeminal neuralgia, a condition characterized by compression of the trigeminal nerve by a small blood vessel, resulting in shooting electrical pain in the face.
The patient has a confirmed diagnosis of trigeminal neuralgia.
Problem list: Trigeminal neuralgia; active.
Plan:
I prescribed Tegretol 100mg daily for the patient's trigeminal neuralgia.
We will monitor the patient's response to the medication and adjust the dosage as needed.
If the patient's pain persists, we will discuss surgical options for decompression of the nerve.
The patient will undergo genetic testing to assess their suitability for the medication.
We will schedule a follow-up appointment in one month to reassess the patient's symptoms and adjust treatment as necessary.
Follow-up:
The patient will return in one month to reassess their symptoms and adjust treatment as necessary.
